g.text: Case Study: IoT Design for Home Automation

g.data-narration: Home automation transforms ordinary houses into smart homes by connecting everyday devices to the internet, enabling remote monitoring, control, and automation.
g.text: Transforms conventional living spaces into "smart homes" by connecting
everyday devices to the internet for remote control and automation.

g.data-narration: We will learn to analyze smart home architecture, understand sensors and actuators, explore communication protocols, and master cloud integration for remote access.
A smart home follows the four-layer IoT architecture: Devices at the bottom, Communication, Cloud Processing, and Application layer at the top.
The Sensing layer uses sensors to collect environmental data: temperature sensors for AC control, motion sensors for security, and light sensors for automated blinds.
Actuators perform physical actions based on commands: smart relays switch lights, motor controllers operate blinds, smart locks secure doors, and solenoid valves control water.
Edge devices like ESP32 and Raspberry Pi serve as local brains, reading sensor data, driving actuators, and handling local communication with the gateway.
g.text: Read sensor data, run local logic,

g.data-narration: The Communication layer uses PAN protocols like Zigbee, Z-Wave, and BLE for low-power battery-operated sensors connecting to a central home gateway.
LAN uses Wi-Fi for high-bandwidth devices like cameras. The home gateway connects to the internet (WAN) for cloud communication.
Cloud platforms like AWS IoT, Google Cloud, and Azure receive telemetry data, apply rules, and host mobile app backends for remote user access.
g.text: Apply rules: if temp >30°C, turn on AC

g.data-narration: The Application layer provides user interfaces: mobile apps for primary control, voice assistants like Alexa for hands-free commands, and web dashboards for detailed monitoring.
Let's build an automated lighting system: PIR sensor detects motion, LDR measures light, ESP32 runs the rule, relay switches the light, cloud notifies the user.
Key design considerations include interoperability between brands, security against unauthorized access, power management for batteries, and user experience with simple controls.

In summary, home automation demonstrates the complete IoT stack: sensors and actuators, hybrid communication, cloud processing, and user-friendly applications for comfort, security, and energy efficiency.

g.text: Four-layer IoT architecture: Devices → Communication → Cloud → Application
Hybrid communication: Zigbee/BLE for sensors, Wi-Fi for bandwidth devices
Edge vs Cloud: Quick local responses vs complex analytics
Benefits: Energy efficiency, security, remote control, comfort
